---
title: "1.2 Common Pitfalls of Backtesting"
subtitle: "Chapter 01. Backtesting and Automated Execution"

format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: show
    code-tools: true
---

# Why Pitfalls Matter

Backtesting errors are not evenly balanced. In Chapter 1, Chan emphasizes that
most pitfalls make a strategy look better than it could have been in actual
trading. That direction of error is dangerous because it converts an
implementation weakness into apparent alpha.

The pitfalls in this section share a common pattern. Each one lets the backtest
use information, securities, prices, or venues that the live strategy could not
have used in the same way. A credible backtest therefore has to ask not only
whether the trading rule is profitable, but also whether the historical test
used the same information set and market access that the trader would have had.

# Look-Ahead Bias

Look-ahead bias occurs when the backtest uses future information to make a
current decision. A same-day high or low price is a simple example: before the
trading day ends, that high or low is not yet known. If a strategy uses that
value to trigger an intraday entry, the backtest is predicting with information
that a live system could not obtain.

This bias is fundamentally a programming error. It can infect a backtest, but it
cannot infect a live trading program in the same form because live code cannot
request tomorrow's prices. For that reason, one strong defense is to make the
backtest and live system share the same strategy logic. When the only difference
between the two modes is the data source, future information has fewer places to
enter accidentally.

# Data-Snooping Bias and the Beauty of Linearity

Data-snooping bias appears when a model has enough free parameters or trading
rules to fit random historical patterns. A formal out-of-sample test is supposed
to catch this problem. In practice, the test can lose its meaning when the
researcher keeps revising the model after seeing disappointing validation
results. That revision turns the out-of-sample data into another training set.

Cross-validation can reduce this problem when the data set is small or when a
single fixed validation period would be too arbitrary. The model is trained and
checked across different subsets, and the researcher asks whether the result is
stable across those subsets. A strategy with a high Sharpe ratio and short
drawdown duration is easier to validate because fewer subsamples are dominated
by long losing periods.

Model simplicity is the deeper defense. A model with few parameters but many
hand-built trading rules can still overfit. Chan's practical preference is
therefore not only fewer parameters, but fewer opportunities to encode
historical accidents. Linear models often satisfy this standard better than
nonlinear models because they are easier to explain, easier to validate, and
less able to memorize noise.

Linearity also applies to capital allocation. In a mean-reverting model, a
linear allocation scales the position with the deviation from the mean rather
than waiting for a hard threshold. In a factor model, a simple equal-weighted
combination of normalized predictors can be more robust than a heavily tuned
set of coefficients. If factors $f_i$ are normalized to $z_i$, a deliberately
simple predictor can combine their signs and standardized values rather than
optimize every weight:

$$
z_i = \frac{f_i - \operatorname{mean}(f_i)}
           {\operatorname{std}(f_i)}
$$

The point of this formula is not that every strategy should use equal weights.
The point is that every extra modeling choice needs a reason strong enough to
outweigh the additional data-snooping risk.

# Stock Splits and Dividend Adjustments

Corporate actions can create false signals if the historical price series is
not adjusted. In an $N$-for-1 split, the share price falls by a factor of $N$,
but the shareholder owns $N$ times as many shares. The market value is unchanged,
so the pre-split historical prices must be adjusted by the same factor. Without
that adjustment, the backtest may treat an accounting change as a trading
signal.

Dividends create a similar problem. On the ex-dividend date, the stock price
typically drops by roughly the dividend amount, while the shareholder receives
the distribution. A price-only backtest that ignores the dividend adjustment can
interpret this mechanical price drop as an economic move. The same logic applies
to ETFs, while options require more specialized treatment.

These adjustments are not only historical clean-up. A live system that uses
historical lookbacks must update those historical prices before the market opens
on the relevant ex-date; otherwise, the live signal can inherit the same false
jump that would distort a backtest.

# Survivorship Bias in Stock Databases

Survivorship bias occurs when the historical database contains only securities
that still exist today. A backtest built on that database can accidentally
assume that the trader knew which firms would survive.

This bias is especially damaging for long-only mean-reversion strategies. A
rule that buys the stock with the largest recent drop can look attractive if
the database excludes the firms that later failed. The surviving stocks are the
ones that recovered enough to remain in the data set, so the test overstates
the long strategy's performance.

The direction of the bias depends on the strategy. For a short-only
mean-reversion strategy, excluding bankrupt or delisted firms can remove some of
the best short trades and make performance look worse. For long-short
mean-reversion, the two effects partly offset, but the problem remains. A
survivorship-bias-free database is therefore the preferred solution; using only
very recent data can reduce, but not eliminate, the damage when better data are
unavailable.

# Primary Versus Consolidated Stock Prices

U.S. stocks trade across primary exchanges, ECNs, and other venues. Standard
daily open and close prices often come from consolidated data, which record the
first or last regular-hours execution across venues. Market-on-open and
market-on-close orders, however, are routed to the primary exchange auction.

This distinction matters most when the trading rule depends on the open, close,
high, or low. A small trade on a secondary venue can print at a price far from
the primary auction price. A mean-reversion backtest that buys after such an
outlier may record a profit when the next price reverts, even though the trader
could not have executed at the outlier price in meaningful size.

The clean solution is to use primary-exchange historical prices for strategies
that execute in primary open or close auctions. If that data source is not
available, the backtest should be treated with skepticism rather than accepted
as a precise estimate.

# Venue Dependence of Currency Quotes

Currency markets are fragmented, and there is no rule requiring each venue to
display or execute at the best bid or ask across all venues. A foreign-exchange
backtest is realistic only when its historical data come from the venue, or the
same executable venue set, that the live strategy can trade.

This venue dependence affects both signals and costs. Trade prices and trade
sizes are often unavailable or delayed, so bid-ask quotes are usually the safer
basis for a forex backtest. Those quotes still vary across venues, which means
that transaction costs and apparent opportunities are venue-specific.

# Pitfall Map

| Pitfall | Backtest mistake | Practical defense |
|---|---|---|
| Look-ahead bias | Use information unavailable at the decision time | Share strategy logic between backtest and live trading |
| Data-snooping bias | Tune rules until validation data look good | Use simple models, cross-validation, and walk-forward tests |
| Corporate actions | Treat splits or dividends as economic price moves | Adjust historical prices before the signal uses them |
| Survivorship bias | Exclude delisted securities | Use survivorship-bias-free data |
| Consolidated prices | Test auction orders on non-auction prints | Use primary-exchange prices for MOO/MOC strategies |
| FX venue mismatch | Test with quotes from venues you cannot trade | Match historical data to executable venues |

# Review Checklist

- Does every signal use only information known at the decision time?
- Was the model changed after seeing validation results?
- Are split and dividend adjustments applied before the strategy uses the
  historical lookback?
- Does the equity universe include delisted securities where the strategy would
  have traded them?
- Do open, close, high, and low prices match the execution venue?
- Do currency quotes and spreads come from venues the live strategy can access?

# Reading Path

- Previous: [1.1 The Importance of Backtesting](../01-the-importance-of-backtesting/web-notes.qmd)
- Next: [Chapter overview](../../index.qmd)
